cases.name: Brown v. Mississippi
cases.holding: A confession extracted by physical torture is involuntary and its use to convict violates Fourteenth Amendment due process.

cases.name: Ashcraft v. Tennessee
cases.holding: Thirty-six hours of continuous, relay interrogation without sleep is "inherently coercive," rendering the resulting confession involuntary under due process.

cases.name: Spano v. New York
cases.holding: A confession produced by psychological pressure — here a friend's feigned distress plus persistent overnight questioning of a suspect who had a lawyer and asked for him — is involuntary; "the petitioner's will was overborne."

cases.name: Colorado v. Connelly
cases.holding: A confession is "involuntary" for due-process purposes only when there is COERCIVE POLICE ACTIVITY; a suspect's mental illness or internal compulsion, without police overreaching, does not make it involuntary.

quotes.text: We hold that coercive police activity is a necessary predicate to the finding that a confession is not "voluntary" within the meaning of the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

cases.name: Arizona v. Fulminante
cases.holding: The admission of an involuntary/coerced confession is a "trial error" subject to harmless-error analysis under Chapman, not automatic reversal (though on these facts the error was NOT harmless and the conviction was reversed).

cases.name: Chambers v. Florida
cases.holding: Confessions extracted through prolonged, incommunicado interrogation of helpless prisoners were the product of compulsion and their use to obtain convictions violated due process.

quotes.text: To permit human lives to be forfeited upon confessions thus obtained would make of the constitutional requirement of due process of law a meaningless symbol.

cases.treatment_note: Current; foundational due-process voluntariness / coerced-confession decision.

note: Phase-3 FINAL authoring manifest: packet(manifest+deck) + P3 gap verifications + marquee quotes + good-law.
